sentences.target_language: 自己紹介をお願いします。
sentences.grammarNote: 自己紹介 (jiko shōkai) = 'self-introduction'. This is the standard phrase used at interviews, first meetings, and class introductions. Japanese interviews almost always begin with this request. Prepare a brief, structured one!

sentences.base_language: I graduated from Beijing University two years ago.
sentences.target_language: 二年前に東京大学を卒業しました。
sentences.romanized: Ninen mae ni Tōkyō daigaku wo sotsugyō shimashita.
sentences.grammarNote: 〜前 (mae) = 'ago / before': 二年前 (two years ago), 一週間前 (one week ago). The structure is time + 前に: 二年前に卒業した (graduated two years ago). Compare with 〜後 (go/ato) = 'after / later': 二年後に (two years later).

sentences.base_language: I have three years of work experience.
sentences.target_language: 三年間の職務経験があります。
sentences.romanized: Sannenkan no shokumu keiken ga arimasu.

sentences.target_language: 私の一番の強みはチームワークです。
sentences.romanized: Watashi no ichiban no tsuyomi wa chīmuwāku desu.
sentences.grammarNote: 一番 (ichiban) = 'number one / most'. It functions like the Chinese 最 (zuì) to form superlatives: 一番好き (like most), 一番大切 (most important). Unlike Chinese 最, 一番 often comes in the pattern 一番の + noun.

sentences.base_language: Why do you want to work here?
sentences.target_language: なぜ弊社を志望したのですか？
sentences.romanized: Naze heisha wo shibō shita no desu ka?
sentences.grammarNote: 弊社 (heisha) = 'our company' (humble). 志望する (shibō suru) = 'to aspire to / apply for'. In Japanese interviews, special humble and honorific vocabulary is expected. 貴社 (kisha) is the respectful form used when referring to the interviewer's company.

sentences.base_language: I'm very interested in this company's mission.
sentences.target_language: 御社のミッションにとても興味を持っています。
sentences.romanized: Onsha no misshon ni totemo kyōmi wo motte imasu.
sentences.literal: Your-company's mission to very interest holding.
sentences.grammarNote: 〜に興味を持つ (ni kyōmi wo motsu) = 'to have interest in / be interested in'. This is the standard Japanese expression for interest, parallel to Chinese 对…感兴趣. 御社 (onsha) is a formal honorific for the interviewer's company (spoken form; written form is 貴社).

sentences.base_language: Do you have any questions for us?
sentences.target_language: 何かご質問はありますか？

sentences.base_language: When can I expect to hear back?
sentences.target_language: 結果はいつ頃お知らせいただけますか？
sentences.romanized: Kekka wa itsu-goro oshirase itadakemasu ka?
sentences.grammarNote: いただける (itadakeru) is the potential form of いただく, the humble form of もらう: 'can I humbly receive…?'. 〜頃 (goro) = 'around / approximately (a time)'. This phrasing is appropriately humble for asking an interviewer about follow-up.
